frameworks.name: evolutionarily prepared learning
frameworks.one_line: innate threat-relevant cues bias associative fear acquisition in humans

frameworks.name: Pavlovian fear conditioning
frameworks.one_line: CS-US association measured via conditioned fear responses to visual cues

frameworks.name: context-dependent memory
frameworks.one_line: olfactory context modulates encoding and generalization of fear memory

core_question: Does a threat-relevant predator odor context strengthen the formation and persistence of human fear memory?
key_assumptions: 2-methyl-2-thiazoline acts as an evolutionarily prepared predator-odor cue in humans
aversiveness-matched control odor isolates threat-relevance from generic unpleasantness
cortisol indexes a biological stress mechanism linking odor to fear consolidation
manipulations: odor context during acquisition (predator odor vs aversiveness-matched control)
test odor context (same vs different) and extinction phase
measurement of cortisol in a separate experiment
key_findings: predator-odor group showed stronger conditioned fear responses to visual CS than control-odor group
enhanced fear generalized to a different odor context at test
predator odor was associated with elevated cortisol levels
interpretations: innate olfactory threat cues can amplify human fear learning
odor-context modulation may contribute to maladaptive fear-memory persistence (e.g., PTSD)
limitations_noted: cortisol mechanism evidence comes from a separate experiment
